blurb: A dense, slow-creeping mat of glossy olive-green leaves that throws up short, chubby spikes of deep violet-blue flowers in early summer. A tough, tidy alpine from the European Alps — a distinct mat-forming speedwell, unlike the guide's upright spike speedwells or the tiny-leaved Turkish creeper.
water: Low to moderate; well-drained soil, never soggy
seasons: Chubby deep violet-blue flower spikes through early-to-late summer; glossy mat semi-evergreen where winters are mild
wildlife: Bees and butterflies visit the flower spikes

care.hardiness: Very cold-hardy — rated roughly USDA zones 2–9 — so an alpine like this shrugs off Boulder's zone 5b–6a winters; cold is never its problem, poor drainage is.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant in spring after the mid-May last frost (or in early fall) on a sunny, sharply drained site — a rock garden, trough, raised bed or gritty border edge is ideal. In our heavy clay, plant it high on a grit-amended mound so water drains away from the crown. It also does well in a well-drained container or alpine trough.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun gives the tightest mat and the most flower spikes; it wants the open, bright exposure of its alpine home.[1]
care.soil: Undemanding about soil type — it takes normal, sandy or clay ground at neutral, alkaline or acid pH — but it insists on good drainage; a lean, gritty, fast-draining mix suits it best.[1]
care.water: Give moderate water while it establishes and during dry spells, letting the top of the soil dry between waterings; once settled it is fairly drought-tolerant. The one thing to avoid is standing moisture, which rots the crown.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space plants about 8–12 in apart for a knit mat; it is a slow grower that stays put, so it makes a neat, non-aggressive groundcover or edging.[1]
care.bloom: Short, dense 'chubby' spikes of deep violet-blue flowers cover the mat from early through late summer, held just above the glossy foliage.[1]
care.maintenance: Almost none — shear off the spent flower spikes after bloom to tidy the mat and it may reflush lightly. The foliage stays evergreen in mild winters but can brown in a hard, dry Front Range cold snap; tidy any winter-burned leaves in spring. The main killer is wet clay, so keep the crown dry.[1][2]
care.propagation: Increase it by division of the creeping mat in spring or early fall, or by soft stem cuttings; it can also be grown from seed. Division is the quick, reliable way to make more.[1][2]
care.troubles: Trouble-free of pests and disease in a dry, well-drained spot; crown and root rot in soggy or poorly drained soil is essentially the only thing that fails it.[1][2]

habitat: Alpine and subalpine rocky slopes and screes of the Alps

edible.summary: Grown purely as an ornamental alpine groundcover — no part is used as food.[1]
edible.caution: Alpine speedwell has no documented edible use; it is a rock-garden ornamental, not a food or herb plant. Don't eat it.[1][2]
